macro_events.event_summary: U.S. and Iran reach a roadmap for a final nuclear/sanctions deal and agree to wind down military operations in Lebanon, signaling major Middle East de-escalation.
primary_drivers: Middle East geopolitical risk premium collapse lowers crude oil prices
Potential return of Iranian oil supply (~1-1.5 mb/d) to global markets if sanctions ease
Reduced defense spending urgency and lower demand for munitions/missile defense

beneficiaries.rationale: Domestic US airline with no fuel hedging program; jet fuel is ~25-30% of operating costs. A sharp drop in crude from removal of Iran risk premium and potential supply return flows directly to margin expansion.
Large fuel cost exposure (~20%+ of opex) and owns Monroe refinery, but net beneficiary of lower crude. Also benefits from improved international travel sentiment to Middle East/Europe corridors.
Cruise operator with Mediterranean itineraries directly affected by Middle East tensions; bunker fuel is a major cost. De-escalation reopens Eastern Med routes and lowers fuel bill simultaneously.
Commodity chemicals producer with naphtha/ethane feedstock costs tied to oil. Lower crude compresses input costs while demand sentiment improves on lower recession-via-oil-spike risk.
Diesel/transportation costs are a meaningful component of retail logistics; lower oil supports consumer discretionary income and reduces freight cost pressure on grocery margins.

losers.rationale: Integrated major with upstream production levered to Brent crude price. A collapse in geopolitical risk premium plus potential 1+ mb/d Iranian barrels returning to market directly compresses realized prices and upstream earnings.
Pure-play US E&P with highest oil-price beta among large caps and significant debt load; earnings and FCF are extremely sensitive to a $5-10 drop in WTI from risk premium unwind.

Prime contractor on PAC-3, THAAD missile defense and Hellfire — systems with surging demand from Middle East conflicts. De-escalation in Lebanon reduces near-term foreign military sales urgency and replenishment orders.
Major supplier of Patriot interceptors and Stinger missiles consumed/ordered during Middle East operations. Reduced regional kinetic activity weakens the FY26+ munitions reorder cycle thesis.
Heavy exposure to missile defense and munitions tied to Israel/Gulf customers. Lebanon de-escalation reduces urgency of supplemental defense appropriations that have been a tailwind.
Oilfield services revenue tracks E&P capex, which is highly sensitive to oil price. Sustained crude downside from Iran supply return cuts into international drilling activity guidance.
Apache has direct Egyptian production exposure and high oil-price beta; loses on both lower realized prices and removal of any Egypt geopolitical premium.

headlines: U.S., Iran agree on roadmap for final deal and plan to end military operations in Lebanon

macro_events.event_summary: China imposed trade curbs (export controls and procurement exclusions) on dozens of US firms in retaliation for the Pentagon's blacklist of Chinese companies.
primary_drivers: Loss of China revenue/market access for targeted US firms
Escalation of US-China decoupling boosting domestic/allied substitutes
Rare earth and critical input supply risk to US defense and tech supply chains

beneficiaries.rationale: MP Materials is the primary US-listed rare earth miner; any Chinese restriction on critical minerals procurement/exports directly elevates domestic supply value. Defense contracts and DoD funding accelerate on each escalation.
USA Rare Earth — pure-play domestic rare earth and magnet supply chain story; benefits from any narrative of China weaponizing critical mineral exports.
Lockheed Martin has near-zero China revenue exposure but is a Pentagon prime contractor; escalation reinforces sustained defense budget tailwind and onshoring of defense supply chain.
Palantir's growth thesis is tied to Western defense/intel decoupling from China; geopolitical escalation reinforces narrative and government deal flow.
Taiwan Semi benefits at the margin as US firms restricted from China procurement seek non-Chinese foundry capacity, though TSM itself has China customer exposure — net positive on substitution.

losers.rationale: Boeing is a perennial top target in US-China retaliation cycles; China is a major commercial aircraft customer and procurement exclusion or order delays directly hit backlog and deliveries.
Raytheon has historically appeared on Chinese unreliable entity / sanctions lists due to Taiwan arms sales; procurement exclusions remove any residual China civil aerospace (Collins/Pratt) revenue.
General Dynamics (Gulfstream business jets and defense) faces direct China procurement bans given its defense-Taiwan exposure profile — Gulfstream loses HNW China buyers.
Qualcomm derives ~45-60% of revenue from China (Xiaomi, Oppo, Vivo); any export control retaliation or Chinese unreliable entity designation directly threatens licensing and chip sales.
Tesla has ~20% revenue from China (Shanghai Gigafactory + China sales); broad retaliation environment risks consumer backlash and regulatory friction even if not directly named.
Applied Materials has significant China semicap revenue exposure; tit-for-tat export controls expand China's 'unreliable entity' procurement exclusions to US semicap equipment vendors.
Caterpillar's China construction equipment sales are vulnerable to procurement exclusions and SOE buyer pressure during retaliation cycles.
Honeywell has aerospace and industrial automation exposure to China and has been named in prior Chinese unreliable entity actions tied to Taiwan defense sales.

headlines: China imposes trade curbs on dozens of U.S. firms in retaliation for Pentagon blacklist
